insights: Ed Gompf manages 76 of 101 assigned IT employees (75.2%), creating a 3.04:1 managerial imbalance that exceeds the CV=0.5 threshold and is classified as SEVERE, directly threatening system user management scalability and resilience.

The imbalance is not driven by geographic or role-specific concentration—Ed Gompf oversees employees across 24 locations and all 5 IT titles, while Mariano Maury covers 13 locations and the same 5 titles—indicating broad organizational consolidation rather than localized misalignment.

Ed Gompf’s CRITICAL risk score (77.10) stems primarily from extreme span-of-control pressure (76 reports), despite high location diversity and title mix; his overload poses immediate operational risk to IT service continuity and incident response capacity.

Transferring 26 employees—specifically 12 Data Analysts, 6 Systems Administrators, 3 Applied Scientists, 4 Software Engineers, and 1 Network Engineer—reduces the imbalance ratio from 3.04:1 to 0.98:1 and lowers Ed’s risk score to 69.8 (HIGH), achieving near-optimal balance without overburdening Mariano Maury.

Data Analysts represent the highest-concentration risk vector: Ed manages 27 (35.5% of his team) versus Mariano’s 4 (16%), making this title the top-priority reassignment target in Phase 1 to rapidly de-escalate systemic overload.

The ‘Unknown’ location category accounts for 14.5% of Ed’s team but only 12.0% of Mariano’s, suggesting incomplete location data may mask latent coordination complexity—this gap warrants data governance follow-up post-rebalancing.
summary: The assessment confirms a severe managerial imbalance in the IT department, where Ed Gompf bears 75.2% of the total assigned workload (76 of 101 employees), resulting in a 3.04:1 ratio and a CRITICAL risk score (77.10)—a structural risk to system user management stability and scalability. This imbalance is not attributable to geographic or title-based silos, as both managers oversee the same five IT roles across overlapping global locations; instead, it reflects an organization-wide consolidation under one leader. A targeted reallocation of 26 employees—prioritizing Data Analysts and Systems Administrators—is projected to rebalance workloads to 49.5% and 50.5%, reduce Ed’s risk level from CRITICAL to HIGH, and restore sustainable span-of-control thresholds. The recommended 3-phase implementation begins with high-concentration titles in Week 1–2, followed by remaining roles and post-implementation monitoring—ensuring rapid risk mitigation while preserving operational continuity. Next focus areas include validating location data completeness and tracking team cohesion metrics during Phase 3.
